subject: ap-psychology
title: Research Methods & Experimental Design
stimulus:
Rayce is exploring the idea of using online videos to sell his line of custom-made skateboards. W hile his friend
is making a video of him performing tricks at a local skate park, a large crowd forms to watch. Each time
Rayce successfully completes a trick, the crowd loudly applauds and yells, showing their approval.

parts.prompt: Explain how Vestibular sense relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description:
The response must indicate that this sense influences Rayce’s skateboarding by providing  a sense of balance (e.g., equilibrium, information about position of the head in relation  to gravity). 

OR 

The response must indicate that disruption of this sense leads to dizziness.

required_elements: Rayce’s vestibular sense allows him to maintain balance as he’s performing tricks.
alternatives.correct_example: Rayce’s vestibular sense allows him to maintain balance as he’s performing tricks.

required_elements: Rayce becomes dizzy doing the stunts because his vestibular sense is out of whack.
alternatives.correct_example: Rayce becomes dizzy doing the stunts because his vestibular sense is out of whack.

wrong_examples: Rayce’s inner ear helps him do well as he skateboards.

parts.prompt: Explain how Social facilitation relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description:
The response must indicate that Rayce will perform his tricks better if there is an  audience present. 

OR 

The response must indicate Rayce will perform less well at a certain trick if he is not very  good at that trick if an audience is present.

required_elements: After the crowd gathers, his jumps are higher, and his turns are tighter because of the crowd’s presence.
alternatives.correct_example: After the crowd gathers, his jumps are higher, and his turns are tighter because of the crowd’s presence.

required_elements: Because he is doing a fairly new trick the presence of a crowd impedes his performance.
alternatives.correct_example: Because he is doing a fairly new trick the presence of a crowd impedes his performance.

wrong_examples: Rayce is very social, so he enjoys performing in front of the crowd.

parts.prompt: Explain how Incentive theory relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that Rayce is motivated to perform because he is receiving  some external reward (e.g., money, praise, applause, cheering, approval).

required_elements: Rayce  rides his skateboard for the crowd because he wants the applause from the crowd.
alternatives.correct_example: Rayce  rides his skateboard for the crowd because he wants the applause from the crowd.

wrong_examples: Rayce likes skateboarding because he finds it fun.

parts.prompt: Later, Rayce puts the video of himself online, complete with bright graphics and fast-tempo music. He quickly gains new customers for his business by allowing people to try his skateboard free for an hour. Explain how Divergent thinking relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description:
The response must indicate Rayce uses or considers many possible approaches to the  campaign. 

OR 

The response must refer to divergent thinking as “thinking outside the box,” innovation,  or creativity.

required_elements: Rayce used multiple approaches to his campaign with both a video and trying the skateboard free for an hour.
alternatives.correct_example: Rayce used multiple approaches to his campaign with both a video and trying the skateboard free for an hour.

required_elements: Rayce came up with innovative ideas like the bright graphics.
alternatives.correct_example: Rayce came up with innovative ideas like the bright graphics.

required_elements: Rayce thinks creatively about how he can sell more skateboards.
alternatives.correct_example: Rayce thinks creatively about how he can sell more skateboards.

wrong_examples: Rayce used bright graphics and cool music.
Rayce comes up with the one best way to sell a skateboard.

parts.prompt: Later, Rayce puts the video of himself online, complete with bright graphics and fast-tempo music. He quickly gains new customers for his business by allowing people to try his skateboard free for an hour. Explain how Peripheral route to persuasion relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate the campaign used attention-grabbing elements.

required_elements: Rayce uses graphics and music in his video to persuade people to buy his skateboards.
alternatives.correct_example: Rayce uses graphics and music in his video to persuade people to buy his skateboards.

required_elements: Customers are persuaded to buy the boards by the cool soundtrack in the video.
alternatives.correct_example: Customers are persuaded to buy the boards by the cool soundtrack in the video.

wrong_examples: Rayce informs people about a study that was conducted that showed that his

parts.prompt: Later, Rayce puts the video of himself online, complete with bright graphics and fast-tempo music. He quickly gains new customers for his business by allowing people to try his skateboard free for an hour. Explain how Reciprocity norm relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that because potential customers are getting something  from Rayce (e.g., one-hour free trial, a sticker, cool hat), they feel obliged to return the  favor by making a purchase or promoting the product.

required_elements: Because Rayce allowed them to try the skateboard for free, customers returned the favor by buying a skateboard.
alternatives.correct_example: Because Rayce allowed them to try the skateboard for free, customers returned the favor by buying a skateboard.

wrong_examples: Rayce was nice and gave customers one free hour with the skateboard, so they reciprocated.
People saw Rayce do cool tricks in the video and wanted to do cool tricks like him.

parts.prompt: Later, Rayce puts the video of himself online, complete with bright graphics and fast-tempo music. He quickly gains new customers for his business by allowing people to try his skateboard free for an hour. Explain how Mere-exposure effect relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate repeated experience to any element of the ad campaign  leads to increased liking or purchasing.

required_elements: The more often that people see the video the more likely they are to buy the skateboards.
alternatives.correct_example: The more often that people see the video the more likely they are to buy the skateboards.

wrong_examples: Customers used the skateboard for an hour, so they liked it more.
As soon as customers are exposed to the skateboard, they love it and think it is so
